# Research Navigator

# Knowledge Standards

Version: 1.0

---

# Purpose

This document defines the quality standards for every knowledge object created within Research Navigator.

It ensures consistency, readability, completeness and long-term maintainability across the entire knowledge base.

Regardless of who creates the content, every topic should follow these standards.

---

# Philosophy

Research Navigator is not a collection of notes.

It is a structured knowledge system.

Every knowledge object should help answer three questions:

• What is this?

• Why does it exist?

• How does it connect to the rest of the research world?

Knowledge should always build understanding rather than simply presenting facts.

---

# Knowledge Quality Levels

Every topic should be assigned one of the following quality levels.

## Bronze

Basic information only.

Contains

- Overview
- Importance
- Applications

---

## Silver

Suitable for beginners.

Contains

- Overview
- Why It Matters
- Prerequisites
- Timeline
- Applications
- FAQs

---

## Gold ⭐

Research ready.

Contains

- Historical Motivation
- Timeline
- Researchers
- Papers
- Applications
- Limitations
- Open Problems
- Future Directions
- Related Topics

---

## Platinum

Publication quality.

Contains

Everything in Gold plus

- Deep explanations
- Comparative analysis
- Case studies
- Visual illustrations
- Knowledge graph links

---

## Diamond 💎

Reference standard.

Contains

Everything in Platinum plus

- Comprehensive references
- Cross-domain relationships
- Research roadmap
- Interactive learning path
- Continuous updates

---

# Topic Standard

Every topic should contain the following sections.

---

## Identity

- Name
- Domain
- Sub-domain
- Difficulty
- Version
- Tags

---

## Learning

- Overview
- Why It Matters
- Intuition
- Key Concepts
- Prerequisites
- Key Takeaways

---

## Historical Evolution

- Problem Before
- Historical Motivation
- Timeline
- Milestones
- Evolution

---

## Research

- Key Researchers
- Landmark Papers
- Datasets
- Benchmarks
- Open Problems
- Future Directions

---

## Applications

Include practical examples.

Possible categories

- Healthcare
- Finance
- Education
- Robotics
- Computer Vision
- NLP
- Cybersecurity
- Manufacturing
- Agriculture

---

## Advantages

Explain

- Why researchers use this concept
- When it performs well
- Real-world strengths

---

## Limitations

Explain

- Current challenges
- Weaknesses
- Failure cases
- Research limitations

---

## FAQs

Questions should be realistic.

Avoid

"What is Machine Learning?"

Prefer

"Why did Machine Learning emerge after Statistics?"

"Why are optimization algorithms necessary?"

"Can Deep Learning exist without Linear Algebra?"

Questions should encourage curiosity.

---

## Related Topics

Include

Parents

Children

Siblings

Cross-domain topics

Future topics

---

## Learning Path

Provide a recommended sequence.

Example

Mathematics

↓

Statistics

↓

Probability

↓

Machine Learning

↓

Neural Networks

↓

Deep Learning

↓

Transformers

↓

Large Language Models

---

# Researcher Standard

Each researcher should contain

- Biography
- Timeline
- Education
- Research Areas
- Major Contributions
- Landmark Papers
- Collaborators
- Awards
- Legacy
- Influence
- Interesting Facts

---

# Paper Standard

Each paper should contain

- Historical Context
- Motivation
- Problem Statement
- Methodology
- Architecture
- Experimental Results
- Key Contributions
- Advantages
- Limitations
- Future Work
- Related Papers

---

# Timeline Event Standard

Each timeline event should contain

- Year
- Event
- Researcher
- Paper
- Problem Solved
- Contribution
- Historical Importance
- Modern Impact

---

# Writing Style

Write as if teaching an intelligent beginner.

Use

- Clear language
- Short paragraphs
- Meaningful bullet points
- Real-world examples
- Logical flow

Avoid

- Unnecessary jargon
- AI clichés
- Marketing language
- Overly academic wording

---

# Bullet Point Standard

Weak

• Optimization

Strong

• Optimization allows AI models to improve their performance by minimizing prediction errors during training.

Weak

• Statistics

Strong

• Statistics provides the mathematical framework for learning from uncertain and incomplete data.

Every bullet should teach something useful.

---

# Timeline Standard

Every timeline event should explain

Problem

↓

Contribution

↓

Impact

Instead of only listing dates.

---

# Knowledge Completeness Score

Each topic should be evaluated.

Example

Overview

██████████

Timeline

████████░░

Researchers

██████░░░░

Landmark Papers

█████░░░░░

Applications

██████████

FAQs

████████░░

Overall

86%

This helps identify incomplete topics.

---

# Review Checklist

Before approving any knowledge object verify

□ Overview is complete

□ Historical motivation is explained

□ Timeline is accurate

□ Researchers are included

□ Landmark papers are linked

□ Applications are practical

□ Open problems are relevant

□ Future directions are meaningful

□ FAQs encourage exploration

□ Related topics are connected

---

# Final Principle

Research Navigator should never optimize for quantity.

It should optimize for

- Accuracy
- Clarity
- Historical context
- Interconnected knowledge
- Educational value

Every knowledge object should help a researcher understand not only what happened, but why it happened and where the field is heading.

---

Version

1.0
